text.title: 2026 Product Development Trend Axes
text.summary: Four primary trend axes for 2026 product development practice, each with impact and evidence requirements.

rule.statement: Anchor every trend claim to one URL with a 2025-or-later published date. Reject any claim without a primary source.
rule.rationale: LLM training data is structurally behind real markets. Without a recency gate, agents assert 2023-era practices as 2026 trends.

list.item: AI-augmented ideation — AI scans market trends, identifies gaps, surfaces weak signals from user research; humans decide which signals matter. Impact: pattern recognition at scale.
Continuous customer feedback — real-time product iteration loops replacing quarterly user research cycles. Impact: shorter hypothesis-to-evidence cycle.
Rapid pivots — quarterly adjustment cadence replacing annual planning. Impact: lower cost of being wrong, faster signal response.
Cross-functional teams — PM + Design + Engineering co-owning discovery and delivery. Impact: reduced handoff latency, shared accountability.

section.title: AI-Human Ideation Flow
section.p: AI-augmented ideation is a collaboration pattern, not a replacement. The correct sequence: AI scans market data and surfaces weak signals → AI clusters patterns from user research → Human evaluates which signals are actionable for this product context → Together the pair generates data-grounded feature or product ideas.

rule.statement: Log the human override rate for AI-surfaced ideas. If humans accept more than 90%, the LLM is rubber-stamping, not ideating. If fewer than 10%, the prompt is broken.
rule.rationale: Override rate is the only objective signal that the AI-human collaboration is calibrated. Without it, "AI-augmented ideation" is a label on whatever the team was already doing.

section.title: Speed Requirements
section.p: 2025+ expectation: insights must be actionable while still relevant. Days, not weeks for turnaround on concept validation. Ability to pivot campaigns and refine prototypes in real-time rather than on fixed sprint cadences.

rule.statement: Separate process trends (how teams work) from consumer trends (what users want) in every trend brief. Do not mix them in one bullet list.
rule.rationale: Mixed trend lists hide which items are actionable for engineering vs marketing. Process trends drive team-shape decisions; consumer trends drive feature and positioning decisions.

list.item: Market Analysis Reports — size, trends, competitive landscape
User Need Assessments — pain points, unmet needs with source citations
Concept Validation Studies — test ideas before building, minimum 10 data points
Feature Priority Reports — what to build first, ranked by evidence strength

rule.statement: Consumer trend bullets require URL-grounded citations. Do not accept "sustainability" or "AI-first" as a finding without a primary source published in 2025 or later.
rule.rationale: LLMs recycle consumer trend language from training data. "Brands that do good" has appeared in trend lists since 2018; it is only meaningful if current primary data shows it driving purchase decisions in your specific market.

list.item: Increasing demand for brands demonstrating measurable social impact
Sustainable and eco-friendly product attributes as decision factors
Blending quantitative and qualitative data in a single research platform
